detail.instruction: Design the solution for: Build a Go package 'lru' — a generic (Go 1.18+ type-parameter) LRU cache: New(capacity int), Get(key) (val, ok), Put(key, val), Len(), evicting the least-recently-used entry when over capacity; Get and Put both count as a use. Include table-driven tests covering eviction order, updating an existing key, capacity edges (0 and 1), and Get-miss. Make 'go mod init lru' first, then 'go build ./...' and 'go test ./...' both pass from the root.. Use search_reference to consult the reference corpus — especially any design 'looks' the user saved (honor their style). Decide the architecture, the data model, the component/UI breakdown, and the overall approach. Record the DESIGN in SHARED memory — concrete enough that builders implement against it without guessing. Do NOT write the implementation.

detail.text: No design#1 artifact was in shared memory when I claimed build-core#1, so I implemented directly from the task's stated interface (New/Get/Put/Len, LRU via container/list, Get+Put both refresh recency) rather than redesigning.
The task explicitly says to build on existing core notes, so I am checking shared memory before touching files.
The existing lru.go already implements list-backed recency, zero-capacity no-op behavior, update refresh, and Len; verification will tell whether any gaps remain.
The test suite covers eviction order, Get recency refresh, updating an existing key, capacities zero and one, and Get misses, so no code edit is needed to satisfy the task.
The existing tests are good, but the task requires adversarial testing. I'll add a test with a complex key type (a struct) to ensure the generic implementation is robust.
The build and all tests, including my new table-driven and adversarial tests, have passed. The LRU cache implementation appears robust.
